name: Sri Lanka Portuguese Creole

allophone_rules.notes: DISABLES the inherited EP Lisbon 'chiado' (coda /s/ → [ʃ]): SLPC has plain coda [s] — no:s 'we' (Hume & Tserdanelis 2002, p. 4). Identity rewrite cancels the inherited rule by id.
DISABLES the inherited coda /z/ → [ʒ]: no coda [ʒ] is documented for SLPC (Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41). Identity rewrite.

REPLACES the inherited EP dark-l id: SLPC coda /l/ after a non-high back vowel is the substrate retroflex [ɭ], not velarized [ɫ] — maal [mɑːɭ] 'bad' (Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41). After other vowels the lateral stays plain (no velarization documented).

Substrate (Tamil/Sinhala) retroflex allophony, intervocalic case: /l/ is realized retroflex [ɭ] intervocalically after non-high back vowels (Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41; cf. əɳimaʟ 'animal', Hume & Tserdanelis 2002, p. 4). Coda case handled by the redeclared PT_CODA_L_DARK. Allophonic only; no phonemic retroflex series.

Same conditioning: /n/ → [ɳ] after non-high back vowels — nɔɔna [nɔːɳɐ] 'woman' (Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41). Conditioned on the phoneme list because the engine's orthographic back-vowel class excludes ɔ.

ancestors.notes: Portuguese superstrate: lexifier from the Portuguese period in coastal Ceylon (1505-1658); the vowel system preserves a Middle-Portuguese-like quality set overlaid with a substrate length contrast.

Tamil substrate: dominant contact language of the Burgher communities of Batticaloa and Trincomalee. Contributes vowel length, retroflex allophones [ɳ ɭ], /v/ → [ʋ], and homorganic NC clusters (Smith 1977; Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41).
Sinhala adstrate: areal reinforcement of the same South Asian features (retroflexion, [ʋ], NC clusters).

notes: Sri Lanka Portuguese Creole (SLPC), spoken by Burgher communities of eastern Sri Lanka (Batticaloa, Trincomalee); endangered. NO STABLE COMMUNITY ORTHOGRAPHY: modern documentation (Cardoso's SLP documentation project) uses a scholarly Latin convention; this spec models that convention (orthography_kind 'transliteration'): Portuguese-style digraphs nh /ɲ/, ng /ŋ/, ch /tʃ/, j /dʒ/, doubled vowel letters for the length contrast (maal, nɔɔna), and IPA ɛ/ɔ for the open-mid qualities where the sources write them. KEY FEATURES: (1) 19 CONSONANTS (Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41): /p b m w f t d s z n r l tʃ dʒ ɲ j k ɡ ŋ/ + approximant /ʋ/ for etymological /v/ (South Asian areal). NO phonemic retroflex or dental stop series. (2) RETROFLEX ALLOPHONES: [ɳ ɭ] for /n l/ after non-high back vowels (maal [mɑːɭ], nɔɔna [nɔːɳɐ]) — substrate transfer, encoded as allophone rules. (3) VOWEL LENGTH is phonemic across six qualities /i e ɛ a u o ɔ/ (long and short; Cardoso, APiCS ch. 41); schwa is marginal per Cardoso, while Smith's later sketches add full /ə/ and /æ æː/ — CONTESTED; the contested vowels are not encoded (null beats wrong). (4) HOMORGANIC NC CLUSTERS morpheme-internally, not prenasalized unit phonemes (li:ŋgu 'tongue', o:ndə 'wave', iɲtʃə 'draw' — Hume & Tserdanelis 2002, pp. 4-5). (5) GEMINATION of voiceless consonants after short stressed vowels (/saːku/ → [saːkku], Cardoso APiCS ch. 41) — documented, not emitted (length of consonants is not written). (6) STRESS is predictable, not contrastive: it falls on the syllable containing a phonemically long vowel, else on the initial syllable — encoded as quantity-sensitive stress with initial default.

sources.author: Hume, E. & Tserdanelis, G.
sources.title: Labial Unmarkedness in Sri Lankan Portuguese Creole
sources.notes: Open-access ROA preprint #545, read in full; nasal place assimilation and NC-cluster data with transcribed examples (preprint pp. 4-5).

sources.author: Cardoso, H. C.
sources.title: Sri Lanka Portuguese (APiCS Online, survey chapter 41)
sources.publisher: In: Michaelis et al. (eds.), Atlas of Pidgin and Creole Language Structures, OUP
sources.notes: Modern documenter's restatement: 19-consonant inventory, /ʋ/, vowel length across six qualities, retroflex allophony (maal, nɔɔna), gemination, quantity-sensitive stress.

sources.author: Smith, I. R.
sources.title: Sri Lanka Creole Portuguese Phonology
sources.publisher: PhD dissertation, Cornell University (republished 1978, Dravidian Linguistics Association, Trivandrum)
sources.notes: Foundational phonology. Not directly accessible; his later sketches (Smith 2013, Survey of Pidgin and Creole Languages vol. 2, pp. 111-121) posit additional /ə/ and /æ æː/ — recorded here as contested, not encoded.

location.notes: Glottolog's representative point for Sri Lanka Portuguese (Batticaloa area).

stress.notes: Stress is predictable and non-contrastive: it falls on the syllable with a phonemically long vowel, otherwise on the initial syllable (Cardoso, APiCS survey ch. 41). Encoded as quantity-sensitive with initial default: the doubled-vowel long nuclei are heavy and attract stress.
